column1: doc,"# InventoryAlert.UI ŌĆö Components & Pages Audit + Enhancement Suggestions
This document scans the current UI project and lists:
- every route/page and what it calls,
- reusable components and where they are used,
- gaps and recommended enhancements (especially paging, consistency, and maintainability).
- always sends cookies (`credentials: ""include""`) to support refresh token flow
- evicts token and redirects to `/login` if refresh fails

- error parsing currently checks `userFriendlyMessage/message/title`, but the API error shape is `ErrorResponse { errors[], errorId }`. Consider mapping UI error extraction to `errors[0].message` (and include `X-Correlation-Id` when present).
All routes are App Router pages under `InventoryAlert.UI/src/app/*`.
- Add paging controls for news (or link to Market page with selected category).
- Centralize toast/confirm patterns (this page already uses `Toast` + `ConfirmDialog`).
- Consider refetch strategy (manual refresh button, or background refresh interval with backoff).
- Add explicit paging UI for `newsPage` (next/prev) and show current page.
- Add a ŌĆ£loading/error stateŌĆØ component instead of `console.error`.
- Use `MarketStatusBanner` here (currently status is fetched and displayed in-page).
- Add page size selector and show total results if API returns it.
- Add ŌĆ£clear searchŌĆØ UX and keyboard navigation to results (GlobalSearch already has it).
- Add visible paging controls for the News tab (`newsPage` exists in state).
- Avoid fetching full watchlist list just to check one symbol (add a backend endpoint `GET /watchlist/{symbol}/exists` or cache watchlist in a provider).
- Add per-tab skeletons and per-tab error UI (only earnings has a dedicated error state today).
- `GET /api/v1/portfolio/positions` (expects `PagedResult`, but does not pass page params)
- Implement paging UI if portfolios can grow (API already returns paged result).
- Provide optimistic UI updates for trade/position changes.
- Add ŌĆ£bulk removeŌĆØ mode for large lists (with ConfirmDialog).
- Replace native `confirm()` and `alert()` with `ConfirmDialog` + `Toast` for consistent UX.
- Add filtering tabs: Active / Inactive / TriggerOnce / Condition type.
- Add inline validation errors (target value semantics differ by condition).
- `GET /api/v1/notifications` (API returns paged result; UI uses `data.items` but does not implement paging controls)

This is a **documentation-only** review of the `InventoryAlert.UI` project:
- Docker approach for running UI + Wiki (recommendations; not implemented here),
- UX/UI enhancement ideas (recommendations; not implemented here).
- Ignoring type/build errors can mask real runtime issues; consider switching these back on once the UI stabilizes.

## 5) Layout + Provider Flow (How the App Boots)
- Injects an inline script to apply theme class (`dark`/`light`) early to reduce theme flash.

- If token exists, connects to SignalR:

- This provider currently polls localStorage periodically (interval) to detect token changes; this can cause extra work/rerenders.

- Attaches `Authorization: Bearer <auth_token>` from localStorage when present.

column1: doc,"# GitHub Wiki Reuse + CI Sync Plan (InvestManagementSystem)
Important note: GitHub Wikis are backed by a **separate git repository**:
This document proposes how to **reuse** that wiki repo and how to **update CI** so changes in this repository can be published to the GitHub Wiki automatically.
No CI changes are implemented in this task (doc-only).
- Wiki source site in this repo: `InventoryAlert.Wiki/` (Docusaurus)
- builds Docusaurus and deploys to **GitHub Pages** (not GitHub Wiki).
- ŌĆ£GitHub WikiŌĆØ = separate, currently not auto-synced
### Strategy A (recommended): Canonical = Docusaurus docs; Wiki = export/sync
- CI exports Markdown to the GitHub Wiki repo.
- Keep `.github/workflows/deploy-wiki.yml` (Pages) if you still want the Docusaurus site.
### Strategy B: Canonical = GitHub Wiki repo; Docusaurus optional
- Author directly in the GitHub wiki repo (via UI or git).
- Optionally mirror back into this repo (harder to automate cleanly).

column1: doc,"# Worker Jobs & Event Flow (Review + Enhancements)
This document contains **review findings and enhancement recommendations** for:
- API ŌåÆ messaging ŌåÆ Worker end-to-end event flow
Per request, this content is kept under `doc/` (not in Wiki pages).
`InventoryManagementSystem/InventoryAlert.Worker/Hosting/SqsListenerService.cs` runs a continuous polling loop (gated by `WorkerSettings.IsPollMessage`), delegating to:
- runs Redis dedup (`SET NX`) + cooldown logic
### 1.2.1 DLQ + retry behavior (what happens today when a message fails?)
This clarifies the **actual runtime behavior** when the Worker polls SQS and message handling fails.
- Each time a message is received but **not deleted**, SQS will make it visible again after the **queue `VisibilityTimeout`**.

- If the main queue is configured with a **RedrivePolicy** (DLQ + `maxReceiveCount`), then once the counter exceeds that value, SQS will move the message to the **DLQ** and it will no longer be delivered to consumers from the main queue.
- Polling: `SqsHelper.ReceiveMessagesAsync` long-polls (`WaitTimeSeconds=20`) and requests system attributes including `ApproximateReceiveCount`.
- Delete-on-success: `ProcessQueueJob.ProcessBatchAsync` deletes the message **only when** `DispatchInternalAsync(...)` returns `success=true`.
- On handler exception, `DispatchInternalAsync(...)` returns `false` ŌåÆ message is **not deleted** ŌåÆ SQS will retry after `VisibilityTimeout`.
- This is the intended ŌĆ£retry by redeliveryŌĆØ mechanism.
**Important caveat: current Redis dedup logic effectively cancels retries after the first failure**
In `DispatchInternalAsync(...)` the Worker sets a Redis ŌĆ£processedŌĆØ key **before** executing the router/handler:
- It is written with TTL 30 minutes using `SET NX`.
- The method returns `false` (so SQS will redeliver),
- but on redelivery the dedup key already exists, so the Worker treats the message as a duplicate and returns `true` (ACK), which deletes it from the main queue.
- ŌĆ£RetryŌĆØ happens at most once (a redelivery), but the Worker will usually **ACK the redelivery without re-processing**, so the message is effectively **dropped** after the first failure.
- Because the message gets deleted on that redelivery, it may never reach the DLQ even if the queue has a redrive policy.
**Additional caveat: manual receive-count cutoff is inconsistent with DLQ**
- if `ApproximateReceiveCount > 5` it logs a warning and returns `true` (ACK/delete).

This is not aligned with the dev queue redrive policy (`maxReceiveCount=3`), and if the queue has a DLQ configured, deleting the message at high receive count prevents SQS from redriving it to DLQ (message is removed instead).
- If a message fails due to a transient downstream error, you currently may not get automatic retries; it can be lost due to Redis dedup behavior.
- DLQ will only reliably capture poison messages if the Worker allows them to be retried/redriven (i.e., does not delete them early).
**Recommended target behavior (so retries + DLQ are meaningful)**
- On failure: do **not** delete the message.
- Configure `VisibilityTimeout` and `RedrivePolicy.maxReceiveCount` to match desired retry cadence.
- Move ŌĆ£processed/dedupŌĆØ markers to **after** successful processing, or store a separate ŌĆ£in-flightŌĆØ key with a short TTL that doesnŌĆÖt suppress redelivery attempts.
- Rule of thumb: never let dedup turn a failure into a successful ACK.
- Avoid manual ŌĆ£receiveCount cutoff ŌåÆ ACKŌĆØ unless you explicitly persist the failed payload somewhere (DynamoDB / DB / blob) before deleting it.
- If you want a manual cutoff, prefer: on cutoff ŌåÆ emit a ŌĆ£dead-letteredŌĆØ event / persist diagnostics ŌåÆ then delete.
- Decide (and document) the retry policy knobs as configuration:
- `BackoffStrategy` (if using `ChangeMessageVisibility` to slow down hot-failing messages)
- The Worker polls only the main queue (`event-queue`). Messages in the DLQ (`inventory-event-dlq`) will sit until a human/tool inspects or redrives them.
- In local dev (Moto), you can inspect DLQ messages with AWS CLI (same endpoint used in `moto-init`):
aws sqs receive-message --queue-url ""$(aws sqs get-queue-url --queue-name inventory-event-dlq --endpoint-url http://moto:5000 --query 'QueueUrl' --output text)"" --endpoint-url http://moto:5000
- Retrying a DLQ message typically means ŌĆ£redriveŌĆØ back to the source queue (AWS console has a Redrive UI) or manually `SendMessage` back to `event-queue` after youŌĆÖve fixed the root cause.
Found in code but not referenced:
Recommendation: pick one supported polling strategy (native BackgroundService OR Hangfire poller) and wire/remove the other to reduce confusion.

These implement a C# internal work queue (`System.Threading.Channels`) pattern, but there is currently no producer calling `QueueBackgroundWorkItemAsync`, so SQS processing is **not** using the internal queue.
### 2.1 Message contract mismatch: `EventEnvelope` vs raw payload
- deserializes `message.Body` directly into handler payload models
- routing can fail even when SQS contains valid `EventEnvelope` bodies.
### 2.2 SQS message attributes are not requested in the poller
- attribute-based routing wonŌĆÖt work reliably even if attributes exist.
### 2.3 CorrelationId propagation is intended, but fragile
- Worker pushes `CorrelationId` into log scope when it has an envelope (`ProcessQueueJob`)
- publishing must reliably stamp `EventEnvelope.CorrelationId` (API `EventService` currently doesnŌĆÖt).
### Chosen direction (per preference): Option C ŌĆö SQS retries + DLQ, with Hangfire DLQ reprocessor
- Let **SQS** handle automatic retries and DLQ routing (visibility timeout + redrive policy).
- Use **Hangfire Dashboard** to manage *manual recovery* by running a **DLQ reprocessor/redrive job** you can retry from the UI.
- Operators get a reliable ŌĆ£buttonŌĆØ in Hangfire to re-run recovery tasks (redrive/replay batches from DLQ after a fix).
### P0 ŌĆö Make event handling deterministic (one contract, one router)
- message attributes are optional (debug / filtering), not required for correctness
### P0 ŌĆö Ensure correlation is end-to-end (search by one id)
- Wire `HangfireJobLoggingFilter` globally so failures/retries are logged consistently.
- Introduce a **DLQ reprocessor** Hangfire job (design described below) to:
- republish/redrive messages back to the main queue,
- and surface failures in Hangfire so the Dashboard ŌĆ£RetryŌĆØ button is meaningful.
### P1 ŌĆö Make DLQ actually capture failures (donŌĆÖt ACK failures accidentally)
Goal: if a message keeps failing, it should naturally reach DLQ; if it succeeds, ACK/delete it.
Key changes required (because current behavior can drop messages before DLQ):
- Ensure ŌĆ£dedup/processedŌĆØ markers never convert a failure into a successful ACK.
- Today Redis `msg:processed:{MessageId}` is written before processing and can cause the first redelivery to be ACKŌĆÖd as a ŌĆ£duplicateŌĆØ.
- Remove/align the manual cutoff that ACKs messages by `ApproximateReceiveCount`.
- The queue redrive policy already controls the cutoff (`maxReceiveCount`).
- If you keep a manual cutoff, treat it as ŌĆ£move to DLQ / persist diagnosticsŌĆØ, not ŌĆ£delete silentlyŌĆØ.
### P1 ŌĆö Make concurrency & rate limits configurable
- Avoid logging large payloads by default (log excerpt + ids).
### P2 ŌĆö Clarify supported polling strategy
- Document the chosen strategy and remove ambiguity.
API->>SNS: Publish EventEnvelope { EventType, Payload, CorrelationId, MessageId }
SQS-->>DLQ: After maxReceiveCount -> move to DLQ
HF->>SQS: Republish or redrive back to main queue
HF-->>HF: If job fails -> Hangfire dashboard retry
## 4.1 Option C ŌĆö DLQ reprocessor job (recommended spec)
This is the missing piece that makes ŌĆ£retry via Hangfire DashboardŌĆØ practical without forcing Hangfire to own the main SQS consumption loop.
- `dlq-reprocess` (recurring) ŌĆö periodically scans DLQ and replays safe messages
- `dlq-redrive-once` (manual) ŌĆö one-off job to replay `N` messages when an operator clicks ŌĆ£EnqueueŌĆØ
- Pull a bounded batch from DLQ (e.g., `max=10..50`).
- optionally check a ŌĆ£replay allowlistŌĆØ (which event types are safe to replay)
- republish back to the main queue (or publish back to SNS depending on your desired fanout semantics)
- write an audit log entry (Seq + optional DB/Dynamo row) with replay metadata
- delete from DLQ only after successful republish (or keep in DLQ and mark ŌĆ£attemptedŌĆØ)
- If the DLQ reprocessor job fails (AWS outage, invalid payloads, permission issue), Hangfire marks the job as failed.
- An operator can click ŌĆ£RetryŌĆØ in Hangfire Dashboard to rerun the recovery job.

- This is a **job-level retry** (re-run the recovery operation), not a direct per-message retry button inside Hangfire. If you need per-message selection, add parameters (eventType filter, messageId list, max batch size) and expose them via a small admin endpoint or a dashboard command pattern.
- record replay attempts per message id to prevent infinite loops
- emit a new `ReplayId` / `ReprocessAttemptId` for recovery runs
## 5) Wiki doc drift notes (for later cleanup)

Wiki docs currently contain both current and legacy naming/flow descriptions (job naming, routing assumptions). Recommend keeping Wiki pages ŌĆ£how to useŌĆØ oriented and referencing this `doc/` review for deeper refactor plans.",337,af2cebc4f95126d5c1d8ee9aa64a32bf,doc\worker_jobs_and_event_flow_review.md,0,290,"worker, message, hangfire, queue, inventorymanagementsystem, retry, inventoryalert, event",1777190302.622038,10,dbcontext iproductservice threshold notification asnotracking hosted service stock price business logic entity framework price drop finnhubpricesyncworker loss application service external api appdbcontext quote market data productservice periodictimer backgroundservice efcore,# Worker Jobs & Event Flow (Review + Enhancements)

column1: Use this checklist to track your team's progress and ensure you're hitting all the key technical requirements from the judging criteria. Good luck!
- [ ] Endpoints are resource-based and follow RESTful naming conventions (e.g., /api/urls/{id}).
- [ ] Self-descriptive messages are used (e.g., Content-Type: application/json).
- [ ] Appropriate HTTP status codes are returned for different scenarios (e.g., 200 OK, 201 Created, 24 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found).
- [ ] Every request contains all necessary information for the server to process it.
- [ ] The server does not store any client session state between requests.
- [ ] The application logic is clearly separated between the client and the server.
- [ ] Caching headers (like ETag or Cache-Control) are implemented on GET endpoints.
- [ ] GET method is used to retrieve one or more resources.
- [ ] POST method is used to create a new resource.
- [ ] PUT method is used to completely replace an existing resource.
- [ ] PATCH method is used to partially update an existing resource.
- [ ] DELETE method is used to remove a resource.
- [ ] GET, PUT, and DELETE operations are idempotent.
- [ ] Endpoints that return lists of resources support pagination (e.g., ?page=1&pageSize=10).
- [ ] List endpoints support filtering results based on field values (e.g., ?status=active).
- [ ] List endpoints support ordering/sorting of results (e.g., ?sortBy=name_desc).
- [ ] The API has a versioning strategy implemented (e.g., /api/v1/urls).
- [ ] Basic authentication/authorization is implemented (e.g., API Key, JWT).
- [ ] Responses include links to related resources or possible actions.
- [ ] Data from incoming POST, PUT, and PATCH requests is validated.
- [ ] Clear 400 Bad Request responses are returned for invalid data.
- [ ] Basic logging is implemented to track requests or errors.
- [ ] The project includes a helpful README.md file.
- [ ] The API is documented using Swagger/OpenAPI.

- [ ] The application can be built and run using a Dockerfile",49,709150e6d23b0349f58b4e7567578cb3,doc\archive\# Hackathon Progress Checklist.md,0,291,"endpoints, used, method, resource, points, server, implemented, application",1759516769.3479264,1,postgresql compose postgres dockerfile container,# Hackathon Progress Checklist

column1: doc,"# Alert Rules ŌĆö Symbol Auto-Resolve

Creating an alert rule should not depend on the user having previously ŌĆ£discoveredŌĆØ a symbol (e.g., by visiting a stock detail page). The API now resolves and persists symbol metadata during alert creation.
- Input `TickerSymbol` is normalized (trim + uppercase) before persistence.
- API calls stock profile lookup during `AlertRuleService.CreateAsync` to resolve/persist symbol metadata.
- If the symbol cannot be resolved, alert creation fails.
- Unit tests cover alert creation paths without pre-existing catalog state.
- Create an alert for a symbol that is not yet in the catalog; verify it succeeds and the symbol appears in `StockListing`.

- **Authentication**: JWT Bearer Token required unless marked `[Public]`.
> **Design Goal**: Minimize external Finnhub calls over time. Every successful Finnhub resolution permanently enriches our local `StockListing` database, making future lookups free and instant.
This pattern applies to **every flow that requires resolving a ticker symbol** ŌĆö search, add to portfolio, add to watchlist, create alert.
A([Client: search query / symbol]) --> B{Find in StockListing DB?}
B -- Found --> C([Return DB result immediately])
B -- Not Found --> D[Call Finnhub /search or /stock/profile2]
E -- No --> F([404: Symbol not recognized])
G --> H[Trigger background: SyncMetricsJob for symbol]

column1: 1. **Publish**: API/Services wrap payloads in an `EventEnvelope` and push to SQS.
2. **Poll**: `ProcessQueueJob` retrieves messages and checks Redis for atomic duplicates (30-min TTL window).
3. **Cooldown**: `PriceAlertHandler` carries a **24-hour per-symbol cooldown** via Redis to prevent alert storms.
5. **Audit**: After 5 failed attempts, messages are acknowledged and deleted to unblock the queue. All failures are logged via `ILogger<T>`.

column3: `AccessToken` (string, JWT), `ExpiresAt` (DateTime, UTC), `RefreshToken` (string ŌĆö delivered as `httpOnly` cookie, not body)
No body. Refresh token read from `httpOnly` cookie automatically.

column1: > **JWT config**: Access token TTL = **15 minutes**. Refresh token TTL = **7 days**, stored as `httpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict` cookie. Refresh tokens are single-use (rotated on each refresh).
- **200 OK**: `AuthResponse` ŌĆö `{ AccessToken, ExpiresAt }`. Refresh token set as `httpOnly` cookie.
- **429**: ""Login rate limit exceeded. Try again in 15m.""
- **200 OK**: New `AuthResponse` (rotated refresh token set in cookie).
- **200 OK**: `{ Message: ""Logged out."" }`. Clears `httpOnly` cookie server-side.
- **200 OK**: `{ Imported: int, Failed: int, Errors: List<string> }`
- **Request**: `TradeRequest` ŌĆö `Quantity` must always be positive; direction from `Type`.
- **409**: ""Cannot remove a position with active alert rules. Delete rules first.""
> ŌØŚ **Cascade Scope (Critical).** Deleting a portfolio position is a **user-scoped** operation only. It does **NOT** touch any global tables:
> - Ō£ģ **Deleted**: The user's position row + all their `Trade` ledger entries for this symbol.
> - Ō£ģ **Deleted**: The user's `AlertRule` rows for this symbol (must confirm first via 409 guard above).
> - Ō£ģ **Deleted**: The user's `WatchlistItem` for this symbol, if present (auto-cascade).
> - ŌØī **NOT deleted**: `StockListing` ŌĆö global catalog, shared by all users.
> - ŌØī **NOT deleted**: `PriceHistory` ŌĆö global time-series data, never user-owned.
> - ŌØī **NOT deleted**: `StockMetric`, `EarningsSurprise`, `RecommendationTrend`, `InsiderTransaction` ŌĆö market data, never user-owned.
> **Rationale**: `StockListing` is the backbone of the symbol database. Removing a position means the user is exiting a trade, not declaring the company no longer exists.
- **404**: ""Metrics not yet available for this symbol.""
- **200 OK**: `List<PortfolioPositionResponse>` (live quote merged with listing data)
- **200 OK**: `{ Count: int }` ŌĆö used by UI navbar bell badge.

> **C1 note**: `LowHoldingsCount` alert evaluation in `LowHoldingsHandler` must join `Trade` ledger filtered by **both** `UserId` AND `TickerSymbol` to compute the correct per-user net holdings. Never evaluate holdings across all users.

column1: doc,"# InventoryAlert.Worker ŌĆö Architectural Review & Flow Documentation
This document provides a technical walkthrough of the **InventoryAlert.Worker** project, detailing its architecture, infrastructure, event lifecycle, and background job logic.

The Worker project follows a **Background Service / Worker** pattern, integrated with **Hangfire** for scheduling and **SQS** for event-driven processing. It shares the same Domain and Infrastructure (Partial) concepts as the API but is focused on side effects, data syncing, and external integration.
The most critical flow is the ingestion and processing of market events via SQS.
- Each message is deserialized into a generic `EventEnvelope`.
- Uses Redis `StringSetAsync` with `When.NotExists` (SET NX) on key `processed:msg:{envelope.MessageId}`.
- If the lock is not acquired, the message is skipped and Deleted (ACK).
- Specifically for `MarketPriceAlert`, it checks Redis for an `alert:history:{symbol}`.
- If an alert was sent recently (24h cooldown), the event is suppressed but ACKnowledged.
- The job uses a `BuildDispatcher()` dictionary to route the payload to the correct `IEventHandler<T>`.
- Calls `DeleteMessageAsync` to remove the message from the queue.
- Does NOT delete the message. It will be redelivered by SQS after the visibility timeout.
- SQS RedrivePolicy handles the movement to DLQ after MaxReceiveCount exceeded.
Managed by `JobSchedulerService` (IHostedService), registered on application startup.

column4: Updates `CurrentPrice` in DB for all products via Finnhub.

column1: While both patterns run in the background, they serve fundamentally different purposes in the Worker's ecosystem.
These are **time-triggered** routines managed by Hangfire. They are responsible for keeping the system's state in sync with external truth (Finnhub).
- **Flow**: `Hangfire Trigger` ŌåÆ `Job.ExecuteAsync()` ŌåÆ `External API` ŌåÆ `Database Update`.
- `SyncPricesJob`: The most frequent job. Reconciles DB prices with live market data.
- `NewsCheckJob`: Periodically scrapes news to populate the `NewsRecords` table.
These are **action-triggered** routines that respond to events happening elsewhere (usually the API).
- **Orchestration**: The `PollSqsJob` acts as a high-frequency (30s) ""Ingestion Engine"".
- **Dispatching**: It deserializes an `EventEnvelope` and uses a `Dispatcher` dictionary to route to specialized `IEventHandler<T>` instances.
- **Deduplication**: Every event must pass a Redis-backed ""Processed Check"" (`SET NX`) to prevent duplicate processing in clusters.

column4: Logs event; intended to trigger downstream notifications.
Persists structured news snippets associated with tickers.

column1: - **Resilience**: Every message is audit-logged in DynamoDB regardless of success/failure.
- **Deduplication**: Redis locks prevent duplicate processing in scaled-out worker environments.
- **Observability**: Hangfire Dashboard integration provides real-time monitoring of retries and failures.

1. **Unified Persistence**: Previously, the API and Worker maintained disconnected DbContexts (`AppDbContext` vs `WorkerDbContext`). These were consolidated into a single `InventoryDbContext` residing centrally within `InventoryAlert.Contracts`.

2. **DynamoDB Unification**: The `EventLogDynamoRepository` and its data model were also moved to `Contracts`, eliminating duplicate SDK code between API log queries and Worker event telemetry writes.
3. **Internal Restructuring**: Event Handlers and background Jobs were cleanly bounded into an `Infrastructure/` namespace mimicking the API's Clean Architecture approach.

- **Interface Segregation**: `IEventHandler<T>` is clean, but the manual mapping in `PollSqsJob.BuildDispatcher` could be refactored to use a Strategy pattern or DI keyed services if the number of handlers grows significantly.
- **Resource Protection**: Add rate limiting to `SyncPricesJob` to honor Finnhub's tier-specific throughput limits.

- **DynamoDB Reallocation**: Currently, DynamoDB is heavily invoked for simple Event Logs. Future optimization is slated to migrate `NewsRecord` out of PostgreSQL and into DynamoDB to better leverage its time-series query performance.",159,8be22a49bfeb786d1f98fa28b1a453a7,doc\archive\Architectural_Review.md,0,296,"event, worker, dynamodb, hangfire, redis, message, flow, logic",1775670503.9039152,1,fluentassertions data access sku loss msft business logic efcore periodictimer dbcontext iproductrepository iproductservice stock productservice xunit unit test application service appdbcontext stock ticker igenericrepository dbset item finnhubpricesyncworker backgroundservice googl hosted service notification entity framework inventory stock price symbol threshold external api quote aapl market data asnotracking price drop arrange act assert,# InventoryAlert.Worker ŌĆö Architectural Review & Flow Documentation

**Current:** Services throw `KeyNotFoundException` for not-found, return `null?` for optional results.
**Problem:** Callers must remember which methods throw vs return null, and exception catching is expensive.
public static Result<T> Ok(T value)         => new() { Value = value, IsSuccess = true };
public static Result<T> Fail(string error)  => new() { Error = error, IsSuccess = false };
public static Result<T> NotFound(int id)    => Fail($""Entity with id {id} was not found."");

This guide provides a detailed, step-by-step walkthrough to build the **Temporal Action Queue** project from scratch using .NET 9.

public Guid Id { get; set; } = Guid.NewGuid();

public DateTime CreatedAt { get; set; } = DateTime.UtcNow;
Create `Data/AppDbContext.cs`. We use a **Primary Constructor** for the options.
**CRITICAL**: Background services are singletons. To use a scoped `DbContext`, you must manually create a scope using `IServiceProvider`.
public class JobProcessorService(IServiceProvider serviceProvider, ILogger<JobProcessorService> logger) : BackgroundService
protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
public async Task<IActionResult> CreateJob([FromBody] CreateJobRequest r)
var job = new ScheduledJob { TaskName = r.Name, ScheduledAt = r.ExecuteAt };
return CreatedAtAction(nameof(GetJobs), new { id = job.Id }, job);
public record CreateJobRequest(string Name, DateTime ExecuteAt);

Initialize the database using the EF Core CLI tool.
Your dashboard will be available at `http://localhost:5000`.

column1: doc,"# Coding Challenge: Automated Inventory Alert System
Build a C# Web API that monitors product stock levels and generates alerts when they fall below a certain threshold.

column4: Timestamp of the last processed alert

column1: Implement a RESTful API to manage your inventory:
- `GET /api/products/low-stock`: View all products currently below threshold.
- Scans the database for products where `StockCount <= AlertThreshold`.
- **Constraint**: To prevent log spam, only log the alert if `LastAlertSentAt` is null OR more than **1 hour ago**.
- Update the `LastAlertSentAt` once the alert is triggered/logged.
Integrate with the **Finnhub Stock API** to:
- Add a property `TickerSymbol` to the `Product` entity.
- Implement a background process to fetch the current market price for each symbol.
- **Challenge**: If the market price is SIGNIFICANTLY higher than the original cost (e.g., > 20% increase), create a ""High Value"" alert even if stock is plentiful.
- **DI**: Remember to handle the **Scoped-in-Singleton** context issue correctly!
- **Clean Code**: Use Primary Constructors where possible.
- **Messaging**: Motor (for SNS/SQS abstraction) and Hangfire (for distributed job management).
1. **Project Initialization**: Build the standard Web API structure yourself.
2. **Concurrency**: What happens if the background job is running while an API request updates the stock? Consider how EF Core handles tracking.
3. **Resilience**: Add a `try-catch` inside your worker. If the service fails to connect to the database, it should back off and try again in 30 seconds.
4. **Distributed Messaging**: Implement **Motor** to push ""Low Stock"" events to **AWS SNS/SQS** topics.
5. **Robust Scheduling**: Move the inventory check job from `BackgroundService` to **Hangfire** to support persistence, retries, and a dashboard monitor.

_Note: Your previous work has been moved to the `/refer` folder for reference._",71,5b52d4fd44c2049d191d7317705253a2,doc\archive\challenge.md,0,304,"stock, alert, product, products, implement, inventory, entity, lastalertsentat",1775670503.924312,1,stock price symbol external api efcore migration stock ticker aapl application service hosted service asnotracking entity framework stock market data database update appdbcontext finnhubpricesyncworker inventory loss periodictimer sku msft googl productservice efcore quote threshold add-migration backgroundservice iproductservice item price drop schema change business logic notification dbcontext,# Coding Challenge: Automated Inventory Alert System

column1: This document summarizes the architectural, security, and performance gaps identified during a deep code review. Inline `// TODO: [Enhancement]` and `// TODO: [Bug]` comments have been injected directly into the source code to track these issues alongside the implementation.

- **Hardcoded Fallback Credentials (`AuthController.cs`)**: If the `Auth:Username` and `Auth:Password` settings are missing from the configuration (e.g., missed environment variables in Docker), the system currently falls back to `admin`/`admin123`. This exposes the application to unintended access.
- *Action Required*: Remove the fallback. Throw a startup exception or return a 500 error if secure credentials are not supplied via configuration.

- **Lost Update Hazard (`ProductService.cs - UpdateStockCountAsync`)**: The method requests the product entity using `GetByIdAsync`, mutates the stock count, and then saves it via `UpdateAsync`. Any concurrent modifications to other properties (e.g., the background background price sync mutating `CurrentPrice`) will be blindly overwritten.

- *Action Required*: Use an intentional update technique like explicit column updating (`ExecuteUpdateAsync`), or introduce optimistic concurrency (`RowVersion` / Concurrency Token) on the `Product` entity.

- **Race Condition in Message Dedup (`PollSqsJob.cs`)**: The deduplication logic uses `GetStringAsync()` to check for previous processing, and then later (outside the critical path) uses `SetStringAsync()` to persist it. This creates a race condition for duplicates arriving simultaneously.
- *Action Required*: Use an atomic operation like Redis `SET ... NX` (Set Not Exists).

- *Issue 1*: FluentValidation does not automatically validate the internal elements of an `IEnumerable<T>` payload at the controller level unless explicitly wired up with `RuleForEach`. The current implementation trusts the bulk payload completely.
- *Issue 2*: There is no guard tracking empty lists (`!requests.Any()`). Passing an empty array executes a pointless database transaction.
- *Action Required*: Implement a manual validator pass or a collection wrapper for validation, and add a quick empty guard.

- **N+1 External IO inside Loops (`SyncPricesJob.cs`, `EarningsCheckJob.cs`, `NewsCheckJob.cs`, `InsiderTxCheckJob.cs`)**: Each Hangfire background worker retrieves all products using `ToListAsync()` pulling an unbounded dataset into memory, followed by executing a sequential Finnhub HTTP API call `await Fetch(...)` per product inside a `foreach` loop. If there are thousands of products, this will lead to unbounded memory allocation, prolonged job durations, blocking threads, and immediate violation of Finnhub's generic rate limits (60 requests/min).

- *Action Required*: Change `ToListAsync()` to database batching, implement a parallel fan-out API caller (`Task.WhenAll`), and employ `SemaphoreSlim` and `Task.Delay` logic to enforce Finnhub's rate limit.

- **Hardcoded Job CRON (`JobSchedulerService.cs`)**: The price sync Hangfire job is scheduled with a hardcoded `""*/10 * * * *""` cron expression (every 10 minutes). It completely ignores the `MinuteSyncCurrentPrice` config parameter available in `appsettings.json`.
- *Action Required*: Inject `IConfiguration` into the JobSchedulerService, dynamically calculate the CRON string based on the configuration value.
- **Hardcoded Cache TTL (`ProductService.cs`)**: `GetProductByIdAsync` sets the cache TTL permanently to 10 minutes.
- *Action Required*: Move the TTL interval to `appsettings.json`.

- **Missing Liveness Probes (`Program.cs`)**: In containerized environments like Docker or Kubernetes, there is no way for the orchestrator to know if the C# application is actually healthy, routing traffic normally, or silently hung in a deadlock.
- *Action Required*: Integrate ASP.NET HealthChecks (`builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()`) and map an endpoint `app.MapHealthChecks(""/health"")`.
- **Inappropriate Logging Strategy (`FinnhubClient.cs`)**: Error logging writes directly to STDOUT using `Console.WriteLine()`, bypassing the highly configurable Serilog provider.
- *Action Required*: Inject `ILogger<FinnhubClient>` and write structural logs using standard `_logger.LogError(...)`.

- **Leaked Capture Variable Warning (`SyncPricesJob.cs`)**: The class constructor injects `cache` while mapping it to `_cache`. However, the variable `cache` is referenced explicitly at line 62, creating a compiler state capture warning (CS9124).

- *Action Required*: Convert the direct parameter reference to the internal `_cache` field reference.",43,172fac1a4d3444652650da8ca5109234,doc\archive\CODE_REVIEW_TODOS.md,0,305,"action, required, hardcoded, configuration, using, cache, code, into",1775670503.9112332,1,postgresql fluentassertions commit dockerfile unit test stock price productservice postgres productscontroller stock hosted service inventory iproductservice rollback finnhubpricesyncworker container dbcontext httpput appdbcontext asnotracking httppost backgroundservice item application service efcore httpget xunit arrange act assert entity framework business logic sku atomicity unitofwork quote compose iactionresult periodictimer external api market data savechanges,# ­¤öŹ Code Review & Technical Debt (TODOs)

column1: doc,"# Pragmatic DDD: File Structure Guide

This document outlines the **Domain-Driven Design (DDD)** structure for the **Automated Inventory Alert System**. Our goal is to apply DDD principles while keeping the project ""Small & Pragmatic"" by consolidating layers into logical namespaces within a single API project, rather than over-engineering with multiple assembly projects.
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Domain/                 # Pure Business Logic (No dependencies on external libraries)
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Entities/           # Core models (Product.cs)
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ ValueObjects/       # Immutable data structures (Price, Stock)
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Interfaces/         # Repository contracts & External service interfaces
Ōöé   Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Events/             # Domain events (LowStockDetected.cs)
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Application/            # Use Case & Logic Orchestration
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Features/           # Functional slices (Inventory, Pricing, Notifications)
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Services/           # Logic that coordinates Domain logic
Ōöé   Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ DTOs/               # Formats for API Request/Response
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Infrastructure/         # Implementation details (External worlds)
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Persistence/        # EF Core, AppDbContext, Migrations, Repositories
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ External/           # Finnhub API Client, HTTP Clients
Ōöé   Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Messaging/          # Motor implementation, SNS/SQS, Hangfire Jobs
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Web/                    # Entry Point & API Configuration
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Controllers/        # REST Endpoints
Ōöé   Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Middleware/         # Global Exception handling, Auth
Ōöé   Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Program.cs          # DI registration & App Startup
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ InventoryAlert.Tests/        # Shadow the same structure for unit/integration tests
- **Rules:** Must NOT reference any external library (EF Core, Hangfire, etc.).
- **Content:** The `Product` entity lives here. It should contain logic like `bool IsBelowThreshold()` or `void UpdateStock(int amount)`.
- **Content:** If a background job needs to check stock and send an alert, the `InventoryMonitorService` orchestrates this by using the Domain entities and Infrastructure interfaces.
- **Rules:** Holds the ""How"". How do we save to PostgreSQL? How do we talk to Finnhub?
- **Content:** Handles HTTP requests, JSON serialization, and project-wide configuration.

1.  **Big Problem Solved (Architecture):** Prevents the ""Fat Controller"" and ""Transaction Script"" anti-patterns. By moving logic into the **Domain**, we ensure the system is testable without a database.

2.  **YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It):** Traditional DDD suggests 4+ separate C# projects (`Company.Project.Domain`, `Company.Project.Infra`, etc.). For a small project, this adds build time and complexity. Folder-based DDD provides the **organizational benefits** without the **overhead**.

3.  **Modular Growth:** If the ""Pricing Module"" grows too large, it is already logically separated in the `Application/Features` and `Infrastructure/External` folders, making it easy to extract into a Microservice later if needed.",67,bd4ea2e9f0c7a97029b79101b835f043,doc\archive\DDD_STRUCTURE.md,0,307,"project, domain, what, solves, content, into, logic, rules",1775670503.9122765,1,asnotracking quote httppost business logic arrange act assert rollback domain driven design iproductservice xunit efcore migration notification commit application service unit test entity framework httpget savechanges efcore loss stock price external api stock schema change data access appdbcontext atomicity market data sku fluentassertions iproductrepository infrastructure igenericrepository productservice threshold domain entity application layer item unitofwork dbset inventory httpput price drop database update dbcontext iactionresult productscontroller add-migration,# Pragmatic DDD: File Structure Guide

When raw telemetry events outgrow Postgres and Redis connections bottleneck:

1. **Replaces Postgres `EventLog`:** A DynamoDB `InventoryAlert_EventLog` table would use `ExpiresAt` (Epoch) for AWS-native auto-cleanup, shedding millions of stale events without requiring massive SQL `DELETE` sweeps.

2. **Replaces Redis Deduplication:** DynamoDB has persistent TTL semantics. Instead of limiting memory in Redis clusters, worker nodes can do a cost-efficient atomic `PutItem` to check if a `MessageId` or `alert:history:{symbol}` exists, naturally expiring stale histories.

3. **Merges Timeseries Metrics:** Rather than maintaining `EarningsRecord`, `InsiderTransaction`, and `NewsRecord` across multiple SQL tables with foreign keys, a single NoSQL table `InventoryAlert_StockHistory` stores it all via composite Sort Keys (`NEWS#2026-04-04`, `EARNINGS#2026Q4`), creating a blazing-fast audit trail view.

column1: doc,"# ­¤øÆ Inventory Alert System ŌĆö Features & API Reference
> This document covers **business capabilities, API endpoints, and user-facing workflows**.
> For system architecture, Docker setup, and project internals, see [`PROJECT_OVERVIEW.md`](PROJECT_OVERVIEW.md).
All `ProductsController` endpoints require a valid **JWT Bearer token**.
- Credentials configured in `appsettings.json ŌåÆ Auth:Username / Auth:Password`
**Business Goal:** Centralized catalog for warehouse staff to manage product metadata, pricing baselines, and stock thresholds.

column4: NYSE/NASDAQ ticker (e.g. `AAPL`) ŌĆö links to Finnhub
Baseline purchase price ŌĆö **never changes automatically**
e.g. `0.2` = alert when price drops >20% from origin
Cooldown gate ŌĆö prevents duplicate alert spam

column1: `GetProductByIdAsync` uses **`IMemoryCache`** with key `""Product_{id}""` and a **10-minute sliding TTL**.
`UpdateProductAsync`, `UpdateStockCountAsync`, and `DeleteProductAsync` all call `_cache.Remove($""Product_{id}"")` to invalidate immediately.

`POST /api/products/bulk` accepts a JSON array of `ProductRequest`. Runs the entire insert inside `ExecuteTransactionAsync` ŌĆö all-or-nothing. Returns `204` with no body to minimize bandwidth on large payloads.
**Business Goal:** Allow warehouse scanning devices to update unit counts without overwriting other concurrent edits.

The full `Product` record is re-fetched first (`GetByIdAsync`), then only `StockCount` is mutated before the transaction write ŌĆö preventing race conditions where a scanner zeroes out a field it didn't intend to touch.
**Business Goal:** Keep `CurrentPrice` aligned with real-world market valuations to drive accurate loss alerts.
3. If `quote.CurrentPrice is null or 0` ŌåÆ logs a warning and **skips** (never throws)
4. Builds a batch of updated `Product` objects
5. Wraps `UpdateRangeAsync` inside `ExecuteTransactionAsync` ŌĆö all-or-nothing bulk update
Useful for analysts to force an immediate refresh before running price-alert queries.
**Business Goal:** Automatically detect and report products whose market price has dropped dangerously below the baseline cost.
ŌåÆ Alert fires if: lossMagnitude >= PriceAlertThreshold
Before raising an alert, the system checks `LastAlertSentAt`:
continue; // skip ŌĆö still in cooldown window
This prevents the same product from generating duplicate notifications within 60 minutes, even if the price remains below threshold.
**Business Goal:** Emit structured domain events to downstream services (Telegram, Slack, analytics) without coupling to the REST API.
Every published event is written to **DynamoDB `EventLogs` table** before SNS dispatch. Logs are query-able by `eventType` with a configurable `limit`.
**Business Goal:** Process incoming domain events asynchronously without blocking the API, with persistence and notification side effects.

column4: Structured log: `­¤Ü© PRICE DROP ALERT {symbol} -{dropPercent:P1}` ŌĆö **Telegram plug-in point**
Persists `EarningsRecord` to PostgreSQL (EPS actual vs estimated)
Safely logs and discards ŌĆö no crash, no DLQ escalation

column1: doc,"# InventoryAlert Feature Audit & Consolidation Plan
The system currently operates as a ""Modern Financial Inventory Ecosystem"" with the following features:
- **Description**: CRUD operations for products (tickers), tracking their properties and inventory levels.
- **Description**: Background jobs (SyncPricesJob) that fetch real-time data from Finnhub.
- **Description**: User-defined rules (AlertRules) triggered by price or inventory changes.
- **Workflow**: Price Sync -> SNS -> SQS -> Worker Evaluation -> Telegram.
- **Complexity**: Very High (distributed architecture via LocalStack/AWS services).
- **Description**: Supplemental financial data fetched from Finnhub.
- **Complexity**: Medium-High (separate data path from core inventory).
- **Description**: Users can ""watch"" tickers without necessarily having them in inventory.
- **Description**: Event tracking for all major actions and structured logging to Seq.
To reduce the footprint (infrastructure and code), I suggest the following:
- **Reason**: These are secondary ""nice-to-have"" features that significantly increase the infrastructure complexity (DynamoDB dependency).

column5: Moved to PostgreSQL; removed DynamoDB repo.

column1: The Inventory Management System has been optimized for a **Lean** profile:
1. **Relational Core**: All critical business data (Products, History, Alerts) is now in **PostgreSQL**.
2. **Simplified Messaging**: SNS fan-out removed in favor of direct SQS polling, reducing AWS footprint.
3. **Unified Model**: Eliminated the redundant `Watchlist` entity; users now ""watch"" products in their consolidated inventory.
4. **Strict Security**: Implemented ownership checks at the repository level; users can no longer access other users' data.

### TC-PROD-04 ŌĆö Get Product By ID (Cache Test)
GET /api/products/{id}     ŌåÉ first call: DB hit
GET /api/products/{id}     ŌåÉ second call within 10 min: cache hit
- Ō£ģ Both return `200 OK` with identical body
- To verify cache: Add a DB breakpoint or Serilog trace showing cache miss/hit
> ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** Cache TTL is hardcoded as `TimeSpan.FromMinutes(10)` in `ProductService.cs`. There is no configuration key for this. Consider externalizing it.
### TC-PROD-05 ŌĆö Get Product By ID (Not Found)
- Ō£ģ `200 OK` with all fields updated
- Ō£ģ Verify a subsequent `GET /api/products/{id}` returns new values (cache invalidated)

> ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** The update currently throws `KeyNotFoundException` if not found, which is caught by `ExceptionHandlingMiddleware` and converted to `404`. But the middleware maps `KeyNotFoundException` ŌåÆ `404` only if explicitly handled. Verify this mapping is set up in `ExceptionHandlingMiddleware.cs`.
- Ō£ģ `200 OK` ŌĆö returns full `ProductResponse` with `stockCount: 50`
### TC-STOCK-02 ŌĆö Set Stock to Zero
- Ō£ģ `200 OK` ŌĆö `stockCount: 0` is valid (product exists but out of stock)

- ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** There is **no validator** for the `stockCount` query parameter in `UpdateStockCount`. The code directly assigns `existing.StockCount = stockCount;` ŌĆö a value of `-5` will be persisted. Consider adding a guard: `if (stockCount < 0) return BadRequest(...)`.
- Ō£ģ `204 No Content` (runs synchronously, returns after completion)
- Ō£ģ Check that `currentPrice` of products with valid tickers has been updated

> ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** The Docker `appsettings.Docker.json` has the Finnhub API key hardcoded: `""ApiKey"": ""d77d58hr01qp6afl79qgd77d58hr01qp6afl79r0""`. This is a **real API key committed to version control**. Even though `appsettings.Docker.json` may be gitignored, confirm with `git status` and `git log --all -- appsettings.Docker.json`. The key should be moved to a Docker secret or environment variable.
- Ō£ģ `204 No Content` ŌĆö the invalid ticker is **skipped** (not an error)
- Ō£ģ Log file contains: `[ProductService] Null or zero price returned for XXXINVALID. Skipping.`
- Ō£ģ After 10 minutes, `currentPrice` updates automatically without manual trigger
- ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** Confirm the `MinuteSyncCurrentPrice` setting is actually read and applied to the Hangfire job cron expression in the Worker's `Program.cs`.
- Drop% = (700-1000)/1000 = -0.3 ŌåÆ 30% loss ŌåÆ **exceeds** 20% threshold ŌåÆ should alert.
- Ō£ģ `200 OK` ŌĆö response array includes the product above
### TC-ALERT-02 ŌĆö Product Below Threshold (No Alert)
- Ō£ģ This product does **not** appear in the alert response
### TC-ALERT-03 ŌĆö Cooldown Gate (60-Minute Spam Prevention)
- Trigger `GET /api/products/price-alerts` twice in 60 minutes for the same product
- Both should return the product in the list (the endpoint only **reads**, it doesn't update `LastAlertSentAt`)

- ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** `LastAlertSentAt` is **never written** by `GetPriceLossAlertsAsync`. The cooldown check reads `LastAlertSentAt` but nothing in the codebase sets it. This means the cooldown gate is **permanently bypassed** ŌĆö it will always pass the `if (product.LastAlertSentAt.HasValue...)` check because `HasValue` is always `false`. Needs implementation.
### TC-ALERT-04 ŌĆö Zero Price Edge Case
- Ō£ģ Product is **skipped** ŌĆö no alert produced (guarded by `if (product?.CurrentPrice is 0
- Ō£ģ `400 Bad Request` ŌĆö `""EventType is required.""`

### TC-EVENT-05 ŌĆö List Supported Event Types
- Ō£ģ Returns all 5 event type strings:
- Ō£ģ Returns up to 5 DynamoDB log entries for that event type

> ­¤öŹ **REVIEW:** Sending an **unknown event type** to `POST /api/events` (e.g. `""eventType"": ""foo.bar""`) is accepted and dispatched to SNS ŌĆö no validation against `EventTypes.All` occurs at the controller level. The Worker's `UnknownEventHandler` will discard it, but it still pollutes the audit log. Consider adding `if (!EventTypes.IsKnown(request.EventType)) return BadRequest(...)`.

column1: doc,"# Alert Rules & Notifications ŌĆö Business Flow Review + Enhancements
Scope: end-to-end ŌĆ£alert rule ŌåÆ evaluation ŌåÆ notification ŌåÆ real-time delivery ŌåÆ user acknowledgementŌĆØ across:
- Performs ŌĆ£symbol discoveryŌĆØ side-effect by calling `IStockDataService.GetProfileAsync` before persisting the rule.
- Uses `KeyNotFoundException` for missing rule (maps to 404 in middleware).
- `POST /api/v1/notifications/test-signalr` ŌåÆ pushes a test notification via SignalR (admin/dev utility)
## 3) Current Delivery Flow (Worker ŌåÆ SignalR ŌåÆ UI)
- If token exists, connects to SignalR hub (`/hubs/notifications`) using `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL`.
- Listens for `ReceiveNotification` and increments local unread count.
- Also calls `GET /api/v1/notifications/unread-count` to fetch initial state.
- Loads active alert rules in batch (`GetBySymbolsAsync`) and evaluates locally.
- Creates `Notification` rows (in-memory list), persists them, then calls `IAlertNotifier.NotifyAsync` for each notification.
- Evaluates rules for one symbol and creates/persists notifications in a transaction, then notifies via SignalR.
- Creates/persists a notification, then notifies via SignalR.
### 5.1 Two competing ŌĆ£sources of truthŌĆØ for price alert evaluation
Both of these can generate price notifications:
- Duplicate notifications for the same rule breach.
- Divergent rule behavior (ex: cost-basis alerts are implemented in `SyncPricesJob`, but not in `MarketPriceAlertHandler`).
### 5.2 Cooldown/dedup rules are inconsistent across flows
But `SyncPricesJob` creates notifications directly and does not share the same cooldown gate.
- User experiences ŌĆ£alert stormsŌĆØ if the same condition is repeatedly breached across sync cycles.
### 5.3 Contract drift between docs and code
Some Wiki execution-flow docs describe SQS publishing for certain alert flows, while current code shows direct persistence + SignalR notify inside `SyncPricesJob`.
This makes debugging hard because operational expectations (ŌĆ£where should I look?ŌĆØ) are unclear.
### 5.4 Notification delivery is not coupled to persistence outcome
In `SyncPricesJob`, persistence (`AddRangeAsync`) and delivery (`NotifyAsync`) occur, but the notification loop is sequential and can fail per user.
In the notifier itself, delivery failures are logged and swallowed:
- `NotificationAlertNotifier.NotifyAsync` catches and logs; it does not throw.
- DB state can show notification exists, but UI never received real-time push (UI should still fetch via API list/unread count, but the user may miss immediacy).
### 5.5 No explicit ŌĆ£notification typeŌĆØ or ŌĆ£severityŌĆØ
- Hard to group alerts (price vs holdings vs system vs news).
- Hard to present consistent UI (icons, colors, filters).
### 5.6 Lack of explicit idempotency key at the ŌĆ£rule triggerŌĆØ level
- but no explicit rule-level dedup (ŌĆ£donŌĆÖt fire the same rule twice for same symbol within X minŌĆØ) when the source is scheduled evaluation.
### P0 ŌĆö Choose one primary evaluation pipeline for price alerts
- `SyncPricesJob` is the sole evaluator for price-based alert rules.
- Remove/disable event-driven `MarketPriceAlertHandler` for price rules (or keep it for a different event type).
- `SyncPricesJob` only publishes events (or only updates `PriceHistory`), and `MarketPriceAlertHandler` becomes the sole evaluator.
- Ensure handler supports all relevant conditions (including cost-basis if desired).
- Ensure event payload includes enough context (`Symbol`, `NewPrice`, potentially `QuoteTimestamp`).
- **Keep both** Hangfire jobs (scheduled) and event-driven handlers for redundancy and comprehensive coverage.
- Requires rigorous idempotency/dedup logic to prevent duplicate notifications for the same breach event across both pipelines.
- One breach ŌåÆ one notification ŌåÆ one delivery attempt.
- Same rule logic regardless of entry path.
- Per `(UserId, AlertRuleId)` (recommended), or per `(UserId, Symbol, Condition)`.
Then enforce it in the chosen evaluation pipeline:
- TTL configurable per rule type (e.g., 24h for news, 1h for price, etc.)
### P1 ŌĆö Add ŌĆ£notification typeŌĆØ (and optional metadata) to support UX
Add fields conceptually (and later in DB if desired):
Even if DB isnŌĆÖt changed immediately, you can standardize message templates and embed structured fields in logs for now.
### P1 ŌĆö Make delivery robust and observable
- Ensure UI always reflects DB truth:
- UI should periodically refresh unread count and/or latest notifications (not only via SignalR push).
- **Manual Reload Feature:** Build a feature allowing the user to manually reload or pull-to-refresh to get the newest notifications directly from the API.
- Add explicit logging fields for each notification dispatch:
- Consider capturing ŌĆ£delivery statusŌĆØ only if needed (usually not needed if polling exists).
### P2 ŌĆö Align docs to the implemented flow
Once a primary pipeline is chosen:
- Update execution flow docs to match it.
- Keep review/refactor notes under `doc/` (not Wiki), and keep Wiki ŌĆ£how to useŌĆØ oriented.
1) **Implement Hybrid Evaluation:** Ō£ģ Refactored `SyncPricesJob` and `MarketPriceAlertHandler` to share `IAlertRuleEvaluator`.
2) **Enforce Deduplication/Cooldown:** Ō£ģ Implemented Redis-based cooldown (`alert:cooldown:{userId}:{ruleId}`) in `AlertRuleEvaluator`.
3) **Build Manual Reload in UI:** Ō£ģ Added ""Refresh"" button in Notifications page and synchronized unread count badge.
4) **Standardize Taxonomy:** Ō£ģ Implemented `NotificationType` and `NotificationSeverity` enums and updated UI to use icons/colors.
5) **Align Error Handling:** Ō£ģ UI `fetchApi` and `NotificationProvider` are now resilient to background failures and synchronized via React Context.

column1: doc,"# Implementation Plan: Centralized Observability with Seq

This plan replaces ELK with **Seq**, focusing on a high-performance, developer-friendly logging experience optimized for the .NET ecosystem. We will implement end-to-end trace correlation from the initial UI/API request through business logic, background jobs, and database interactions.
- **Structured-First**: No string interpolation in logs; use property templates (`""User {UserId} logged in""`) for indexing.
- **Trace Continuity**: A single `CorrelationId` must bridge the gap between HTTP requests and asynchronous SQS/Hangfire jobs.
- **Contextual Enrichment**: Logs should ""know"" where they are (Service, Environment, Version) without manual boilerplate.
We will use a lightweight, single-node Seq instance:
- **Seq Server**: ingestion via HTTP/CLEF (Compact Log Event Format).
- **Serilog.Sinks.Seq**: Native .NET sink for efficient, buffered shipping.
- **Docker Integration**: Seq runs as a container in `docker-compose.yml`.
- [ ] **Correlation Middleware**: Generate or extract `X-Correlation-ID` from headers.
- [ ] **LogContext Enrichment**: Use `Serilog.Context.LogContext.PushProperty(""CorrelationId"", cid)` at the start of every request.
- [ ] **Request Logging**: Log the incoming path, method, and User ID (from JWT).
- [ ] **Global Error Capture**: Middleware must capture unhandled exceptions and log them with `Log.Error(ex, ""Request failed"")`.
- [ ] **Method Parameters**: Log critical entry points with structured arguments (e.g., `Log.Debug(""Processing alert for {Symbol}"", symbol)`).
- [ ] **Performance Timings**: (Optional) Use `SerilogTimings` for high-value business operations.
- [ ] **Business Events**: Log result outcomes (Success/Failure/Ignored) rather than just ""Method Finished"".
- [ ] **Job Scoping**: The Worker `SqsDispatcher` must re-hydrate the `LogContext` using the ID from the envelope before calling the processor.
- [ ] **Retry Tracking**: Log the `ReceiveCount` to track problematic messages in Seq.
- [ ] **EF Core Interceptors**: Configure `LogTo(Log.Information)` or an `ItemInterceptor` to tag DB commands with the current `CorrelationId`.
- [ ] **Transaction Boundary**: Log when transactions begin and commit/roll back.

column1: This document breaks down the execution steps and dependencies of all background jobs in `InventoryAlert.Worker`.
**Purpose:** Fetches real-time price quotes for all active products.
1. **Fetch Symbols:** Retrieves all product tickers from `IStockListingRepository`.
2. **Parallel Fetch:** Iterates through tickers and calls `IFinnhubClient.GetQuoteAsync(symbol)` in parallel (limited concurrency).
3. **Record History:** Collects all quotes and performs a bulk insert via `AddRangeAsync` into `PriceHistory`.
- Fetches all active `AlertRules` for the processed symbols in a single batch.
- Evaluates rules with a memoized trade basis cache to avoid redundant queries.
5. **Notify:** Performs bulk insert of notifications and dispatches external alerts via `IAlertNotifier`.
6. **Persistence:** Persists all state changes in a single transaction via `SaveChangesAsync`.
**Purpose:** Processes generic background tasks from a local concurrent queue.
2. **Execute:** Invokes the work item within a scoped `IServiceProvider`.
**Purpose:** Bridges AWS SQS messages to the internal processing queue.
- Deletes message from SQS upon successful enqueue.
2. **Batch Delete:** Executes bulk delete on `PriceHistory` records older than the threshold.

3. **Rationale:** With a 15-minute sync interval for 1,000+ symbols, the table grows by ~35M rows/year. Cleanup prevents unbounded storage growth and maintains index performance for historical charts (which capped at 1-year range).
**Purpose:** Consolidated job for fetching both Global Market News and Symbol-Specific Company News.
1. **Market News:** Iterates through global categories (General, Forex, Crypto, Merger) and persists to `IMarketNewsDynamoRepository`.
- Fetches symbol-specific news in parallel (limited concurrency).
3. **Storage:** Uses DynamoDB for high-volume append-only data.
**Purpose:** Syncs latest market news for tracked symbols.
3. **Filter:** Removes duplicates already present in the local database.
**Purpose:** Syncs fundamental metrics (P/E ratio, Market Cap, etc.).
3. **Update Entity:** Maps metrics to the `Product` entity fundamental fields.
**Purpose:** Tracks historical and estimated earnings data.
1. **Iterate Tickers:** Standard loop through active symbols.
2. **Aggregate:** Stores the latest ""Strong Buy"", ""Hold"", etc., counts.
3. **Archive:** Keeps a history of recommendation shifts.
3. **Alert:** (If configured) Enqueues an alert if a high-volume insider sale occurs.

column1: This document outlines the architectural plan for integrating real-time notifications into the InventoryAlert system using SignalR and a Redis backplane.
- **Real-time UI Updates:** Push price alerts and system notifications directly to the frontend without manual refresh.
- **Cross-Service Communication:** Allow the `InventoryAlert.Worker` (which detects alerts) to trigger SignalR messages on the `InventoryAlert.Api` (which hosts the hub).
- **Secure Delivery:** Ensure users only receive notifications intended for them using JWT authentication.
Why do we need this complex setup? Here is the step-by-step business logic:
1.  **The Trigger (External):** The market changes. Finnhub provides a new price for `AAPL`.
2.  **The Detector (Worker):** The `SyncPricesJob` in the **Worker Service** fetches this price. It runs the business rules (e.g., ""Did it drop 10% from the user's cost?"").
3.  **The Decision (Worker):** The Worker identifies a ""Breach"". It creates a `Notification` record in **PostgreSQL** so the user has a permanent history of the alert.
4.  **The Hand-off (Worker -> Redis):** The Worker doesn't know who is currently online. It publishes a ""Real-time Signal"" to the **Redis Backplane**.
5.  **The Relay (Api):** The **Api Service** (which hosts the SignalR Hub) is subscribed to Redis. It sees the alert and checks: ""Is User A currently connected to my WebSocket?"".
6.  **The Delivery (Api -> UI):** If User A is online, the Api pushes the message through the open WebSocket tunnel instantly.

7.  **The Experience (UI):** The user's browser receives the message. A **Toast notification** pops up, and the **Notification Badge** in the header increments without the user needing to refresh the page.
### Why not just use the API for everything?

- **Separation of Concerns:** The API is for fast User interactions (browsing, buying, settings). The Worker is for heavy, scheduled ""Intelligence"" tasks (syncing 1,000s of prices, calculating complex math).
- **Scalability:** If we have 10,000 users, we can add more API instances to handle traffic. All instances stay synced through Redis.
- Create `INotificationHub` interface in `InventoryAlert.Domain` for type-safe client methods.
- Define a shared constant for the Hub route (e.g., `/hubs/notifications`).
- Add `[Authorize]` attribute to ensure only authenticated users connect.
- Update `JwtBearer` configuration to handle SignalR access tokens from query strings (standard for WebSockets).
- In `Program.cs`: Add SignalR services and Redis backplane (even though it doesn't host the hub).
- Auto-increment notification badge in the navbar.
- (Optional) Trigger sound or browser desktop notification.
Since WebSockets don't support custom headers easily, we must handle the token in the query string:
2. [ ] Setup Redis Backplane in both services.

5. column1: doc,"# Hackathon Challenge: The Perfect API
**Theme:** Design, build, and deploy the ""perfect"" REST API based on industry best practices.
**Technology Stack:** C# with ASP.NET Core Web API

Welcome, developers! Your challenge today is not just to build a functional API, but to build a *well-designed* one. We're moving beyond complex business logic to focus purely on the **technical principles** that make APIs scalable, maintainable, and a pleasure to use.
The provided diagram on REST API Design is your guide and your rulebook. The team that best implements the core principles and features shown in the diagram will be the winner.
To sharpen the focus on technical implementation over business logic, this challenge is centered around a **single entity**. You still have two options to choose from:
- The core resource is a ""shortened URL"" which has an original URL, a generated short code, a creation date, and a click count.

- Your goal is to design the API endpoints for creating, retrieving, listing, and deleting these URLs from the ground up, following REST principles. Think about how to handle the redirect logic for a short code.
- We will provide you with a sample .NET project for a simple ""To-Do List"" API (with just one ""Todo"" entity) that is poorly designed and non-RESTful.

- Your task is to refactor this ""legacy"" API, correct its flaws, and implement proper RESTful design patterns. You will need to fix endpoint naming, use HTTP methods correctly, implement proper status codes, and add essential features.
Your project will be judged based on how many concepts from the REST API Design diagram you successfully implement.

- **Uniform Interface (15 pts):** Are your resources clearly identified? Are your endpoints resource-based (e.g., `/api/urls/{id}`)? Are you using self-descriptive messages (e.g., correct JSON media types and HTTP status codes)?
- **Stateless (10 pts):** Does each request from the client contain all the information needed to understand and process it? The server should not store any client context between requests.
- **Client-Server (10 pts):** Is there a clear separation of concerns between the UI (client) and the data storage (server)?
- **Cacheable (5 pts):** Have you implemented caching headers (like `ETag` or `Cache-Control`) on appropriate GET endpoints?
- Are your methods idempotent where they should be (GET, PUT, DELETE)?
- **Pagination (10 pts):** For endpoints that return a list of resources, have you implemented paging (e.g., using `?page=1&pageSize=10`)?
- **Filtering & Ordering (10 pts):** Can clients filter or sort the results (e.g., `?clicks=0&sortBy=created_desc`)?
- **Versioning (5 pts):** How are you handling API versioning (e.g., `/api/v1/urls`)?
- **Security & Auth (5 pts):** Have you implemented basic security measures? This could be API Key authentication, JWT, or another simple auth mechanism.
- **HATEOAS (Hypermedia) (5 pts):** Do your resource representations include links to related actions or resources? (This is a key principle for a ""perfect"" API).
- **Input Validation (5 pts):** Are you validating incoming data and returning clear `400 Bad Request` errors?
- **Documentation:** A simple `README.md` or a generated Swagger/OpenAPI page that is clean and easy to understand.
- **Containerization:** The application is fully runnable via a Dockerfile.
2. Choose your path: Greenfield or Brownfield.
3. Clone the starter repository (if provided for the brownfield option).
4. Design your resource models and API endpoints. **Plan before you code!**
5. Start building! Use the `dotnet new webapi` template to get going quickly.
7. Prepare a short (5-minute) demo for the judges.

Good luck, and may the best API win!",73,16aed27a12944f551ffc88c2424a06d7,doc\archive\Untitled-1.md,0,362,"your, resource, design, endpoints, build, from, resources, challenge",1759516766.1091347,1,appdbcontext dbset data access igenericrepository asnotracking iproductrepository postgres efcore entity framework postgresql dbcontext compose dockerfile container,"# Hackathon Challenge: The Perfect API

column1: This plan outlines the structure and content for a centralized Wiki/Documentation repository designed to onboarding new developers, provide clarity for users, and optimize context for AI coding agents.
Create a single source of truth that defines the technical and functional landscape of the **InventoryAlert** ecosystem.
- **Frontend**: Next.js 15, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS (v4), React Server Components.
- **Infrastructure**: PostgreSQL, Seq (Logging), Hangfire (Scheduler), SNS/SQS (Event Bus), Docker.
- **Tools**: VS Code/Visual Studio, BM25 Search (Agent), GitHub Actions.
- **Database**: PostgreSQL schema definitions and relationship maps.
- **Worker Data**: DynamoDB/Local cache entities for price/news tracking.
- **Authentication**: JWT issues, Token Storage, Protected Routes.
- **Price Sync**: Scheduled tasks vs. Manual triggers.
- **Hangfire Dashboard**: How to monitor internal scheduled tasks.

Market Pulse is an **event-driven stock intelligence platform** that:
- Aggregates financial data from Finnhub (quotes, news, profiles, earnings, recommendations)
- Delivers insights through a **Web UI**, a **Telegram bot**, and **push notifications**
- Reacts to market events asynchronously through a job/event pipeline
- Supports **stocks and crypto** (user-selected symbols from a crawled Finnhub list)
- Centralizes logs through an OpenTelemetry ŌåÆ ELK pipeline
This is **NOT** just an alert system. Alerts are one output channel among several.
Stop using raw domain state names as events. Events describe **something that happened**, not a data dump.

- **Engine**: ASP.NET Core Identity with Entity Framework Core (Postgres).
- **Format**: JWT Bearer tokens for API/UI communication.
- **Roles**: `User` (Subscribed symbols, alert rules) and `Admin` (Job management, system health).
- **Features**: Registration, Login, and Password Reset (simulated via logs).
- **Tool**: Use `Bogus` library to generate realistic financial entities.
- **Coverage**: Initial 50 NASDAQ/NYSE symbols, company profiles, and 3-month sample price history.
- **Environment**: Seed logic runs automatically in `Development` and `Docker` environments if the database is empty.
- **Global Exceptions**: Implement `IProblemDetailsService` (ASP.NET Core 8+) or a custom Middleware to return RFC 7807 `ProblemDetails` for all unhandled errors.
- Use Serilog `LogContext.PushProperty` to attach `JobId` or `Symbol` to every log line in the worker handlers.
- Implement a `DiagnosticEventListener` for EF Core to log slow queries (>500ms) as Warnings.

doc,"# 04 ŌĆö Jobs, Workers & Telegram Bot Plan

column5: Writes to Redis + DynamoDB price-history; publishes `stock.price.updated`; evaluates AlertRules ŌåÆ `stock.price.alert.crossed`
Deduplicates by FinnhubId, publishes `stock.news.published` for new articles
Populates reference list in Redis for UI dropdown
Syncs upcoming earnings to Redis (7-day window)

column1: All jobs (Hangfire & SQS Handlers) must follow a unified result-oriented execution pattern to ensure consistent logging and failure handling.
public enum JobStatus { Success, Failed, Skipped, PartiallySucceeded }
// 1. Validation Logic (Return Skipped if no work)
To avoid scattered API keys and 429 Errors, all Finnhub calls are routed through a `FinnhubClient` with global rate-limiting.
- **Circuit Breaker**: Trips if Finnhub returns >5 consecutive 5xx errors.
- **Rate-limit Guard**: Uses a SempahoreSlim(60) to block concurrent calls beyond the free-tier limit.
- **Fail-Safe**: If call fails, return `JobResult.Failed` but do NOT crash the worker loop.
> Always add jitter between symbol iterations: `await Task.Delay(Random.Shared.Next(100, 400), ct)`

column4: Write to DynamoDB `inventory-news`, notify subscribed users
Write to DynamoDB `inventory-earnings`, notify via Telegram
Trigger immediate profile + quote sync for new symbol
Load rule into Redis for fast evaluation by SyncPricesJob

column1: Retry logic is managed by the `SqsDispatcher` (Path B) or Hangfire (Path A).
- **Retry Count**: Exactly 3 attempts (tracked via `ApproximateReceiveCount` SQS attribute).
- If `< 3 retries`: Let SQS Re-drive (Visibility Timeout).
- If `>= 3 retries`: Move to `inventory-event-dlq` and log ""Poison Message"".
- **DLQ Alarm**: Alert sent via Telegram if DLQ count > 0.
Telegram Webhook ŌåÆ POST /telegram/webhook (internal route in Worker)
ŌåÆ sends reply via Telegram Bot API
- Worker exposes: `POST /telegram/webhook` (internal, not on public `api` port)
- Session state stored in Redis: `telegram:session:{chatId}` TTL 1h
- Rate limit Telegram replies: max 1 message/second per chat
All entities follow the same pattern as `NewsDynamoEntry`:

column1: - Header: market status badge (open/closed), current time
- **MarketNewsFeed**: right panel, latest 10 general news items
1. **Overview** ŌĆö Profile card (logo, name, industry, market cap, IPO date, website)
2. **Price** ŌĆö Line chart (1D / 1W / 1M / 3M from DynamoDB price history) + current quote card
3. **News** ŌĆö Paginated company news feed with thumbnail, headline, source, date
4. **Recommendations** ŌĆö Stacked bar by period (StrongBuy / Buy / Hold / Sell / StrongSell)
5. **Earnings** ŌĆö Bar chart: Actual vs Estimate EPS per quarter + surprise % line
- Market news feed (all categories with filter)
- Rule history: last triggered at, trigger count
- Filterable table: EventType, Status, date range
- Service status cards: API, Worker, PostgreSQL, Redis, DynamoDB, SQS/SNS
- Log level distribution chart (Info / Warn / Error per hour)

All sensitive tokens (JWT, API host) stay server-side. Client sees only UI data.

doc,"# Integration Testing Framework: Implementation Guide
This guide provides a step-by-step blueprint for building a professional-grade integration testing framework for the **InventoryAlert.Api** and **InventoryAlert.Worker**.
The `InventoryAlert.IntegrationTests` project must reference all core layers:
We need a central class to manage the lifecycle of Docker containers to avoid starting them for every single test.
- **Responsibility**: Start Postgres container once per test session.
4. Handle `DisposeAsync()` to kill the container at the end.
This is the bridge between your test code and the running API in memory.
- **Attach Test DB**: Register the DbContext using the `SharedDatabaseFixture` connection string.
- **Mock External APIs**: Replace `IFinnhubClient` with a mock or a controlled stub.
- **Auth Bypass**: Optionally add a `TestAuthHandler` to simulate authenticated headers without hitting a real identity provider.
Testing background jobs requires verifying both the **trigger** (Hangfire) and the **execution** (SQS logic).
- Verify that when a product is created, a message is sent to the `LocalStack` SQS queue.
- Assert using `aws-sdk` inside the test to peek at the queue.
- Instantiate the Job class (e.g., `PriceSyncJob`) directly via the Service Provider.
- Assert side effects in the database (e.g., `PriceHistory` entries created).
Every integration test should inherit from a `BaseIntegrationTest` to ensure a consistent clean state.
- **`ExecuteInScope`**: A helper to run logic within a fresh `IServiceScope` (crucial for verifying persistence).
To maintain a scalable test suite, we follow a strict directory and naming hierarchy.
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Helpers/                  # Auth factories, Data generation (TestData.cs)
Ōöé   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Api/                  # API endpoint tests (ProductApiTests.cs)
Ōöé   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Worker/               # Background job tests (PriceSyncJobTests.cs)
- **Scopes**: Use `ExecuteInScope` for any direct DB assertions to ensure transaction isolation.

Intelligent database cleaner that resets data without dropping tables.
Used to stub external services like Finnhub or SMTP providers.

column1: - Uses `service-containers` or simply rely on Testcontainers (it works in most CI environments with Docker).

> **Data Seed Note**: Do not rely on production seed data. Welcome to the **InventoryAlert** System Wiki ŌĆö the single source of truth for architecture, operational runbooks, and API contracts.

InventoryAlert is a real-time stock portfolio and alerting ecosystem built on **.NET 10** and **Next.js 15**. It tracks global stock prices via Finnhub, evaluates user-defined Alert Rules, and delivers in-app notifications when thresholds are crossed.

> **v2 Note**: The system has been fully refactored from an inventory-management domain to a finance-domain vocabulary. `Product` ŌåÆ `StockListing`, `StockTransaction` ŌåÆ `Trade`, Telegram ŌåÆ In-App Notifications.

column2: ­¤ÜĆ InventoryAlert Documentation (v1) Welcome to the InventoryAlert System Wiki ŌĆö

column1: > A real-time stock portfolio and alert system built on .NET 10 + Next.js 15.

InventoryAlert is a full-stack system that monitors global stock prices via the **Finnhub API** and automatically dispatches per-user in-app alerts when user-defined thresholds are triggered. It is designed to be multi-user, event-driven, and observable by default.
Users who maintain a portfolio of stock positions need to know immediately when prices hit critical levels ŌĆö without constantly checking manually. InventoryAlert automates this through:
- **Background jobs** that sync market prices every 15 minutes across the full catalog.
- **Event-driven fan-out** that evaluates each user's thresholds independently.
- **In-app Notification hub** that delivers real-time alerts the moment a threshold is breached.
- **Full audit trails** so every trade and price movement is traceable via structured Seq logs.

column3: Browse and search `StockListing` entries (DB-first with Finnhub fallback)
Track positions via `Trade` ledger (Buy/Sell/Dividend/Split) with dynamic cost-basis
Watch-only tracking without requiring an open position
Automatic global price updates from Finnhub every 15 minutes ŌåÆ `PriceHistory`
Basic Financials, Earnings Surprises, Analyst Recommendations, Insider Transactions
Auto-fetches Ticker metadata (Logo, Industry, Website, Exchange) on demand
Bell-badge hub ŌĆö breaches write `Notification` rows; UI polls every 30s
Earnings release calendar, IPO calendar, and exchange holiday list
Per-ticker and general news from Finnhub, stored permanently in DynamoDB
Per-user portfolio/alerts/watchlist fully isolated; global market data shared

column1: InventoryAlert is a real-time stock alerting and portfolio management system. The diagram below shows how it interacts with external systems and internal services.
To improve maintainability and reduce project-reference overhead, the solution uses a **Lean Vertical Slice** architecture since v2.

- **Consolidated Business Logic**: There is no separate `Application` layer. Services live in `InventoryAlert.Api` and are grouped by feature slice (e.g., `StockDataService`, `AlertRuleService`, `PortfolioService`, `WatchlistService`).

- **Repository Strategy**: Repositories are feature-specific rather than purely generic. `StockListingRepository` encapsulates EF Core queries, `TradeRepository` computes net holdings via `SUM(Buy) - SUM(Sell)`.
- **Observability-first**: All events flow through **Seq** (structured logging) with correlation IDs. No relational `SystemEvent` tables ŌĆö keeps the DB clean.

column4: Applied at Web layer; Application layer trusts validated inputs

column1: **Why**: Finnhub enforces strict rate limits (~60 req/min on free tier). When `SyncPricesJob` processes 50+ tickers, without caching each ticker would hit Finnhub on every run. With the 30-second cache, rapid successive requests (e.g., from UI + Worker simultaneously) serve from Redis.
**Why**: `ProcessQueueJob` uses Redis atomic `SET NX` to guarantee each SQS message is processed exactly once, even if SQS delivers it multiple times (at-least-once delivery guarantee).
**Why**: Prevents the same symbol's price breach from flooding users with repeated notifications within a short window. `PriceAlertHandler` checks this key before writing a `Notification` row.
### 4. Finnhub Rate Limit Guard (Redis, rolling 60s window)
**Why**: Free-tier Finnhub allows 60 requests/minute. The guard cap is set at **55 rpm** to leave a buffer. Any worker that exceeds this count skips the Finnhub call and uses local data instead.
